paper.title: Hypervelocity Stars in the Gaia era. Revisiting the most extreme stars from the MMT survey

extraction.summary: Paper reanalyzes 40 B-type stars from the MMT HVS survey, obtaining spectrophotometric distances and performing full 6D kinematic analysis for 37 stars. 25 stars are treated as possibly unbound from the Milky Way: the 22 HVS-numbered stars (HVS1, HVS4–HVS24) from the original unbound sample, plus three originally bound runaway stars (B485, B1085, B576) whose status becomes ambiguous or unbound in the lighter Galactic mass model (Model II) or under alternative evolutionary assumptions. The remaining 15 B-numbered stars are concluded to be likely bound and are excluded.

method_chain.summary: Input data: MMT HVS survey flux-calibrated spectra (Warren Brown), Gaia DR2 astrometry and photometry, HST proper motions for 15 stars, and multi-band photometric catalogs (GALEX, SDSS, Pan-STARRS1, Gaia, UKIDSS, VISTA, SkyMapper, etc.).

source_refs.context: In this work, which we regard as Paper~III in this series, we extend the spectrophotometric analysis to 40 out of the 42 high-velocity outliers of the MMT HVS sample.
Photometric data were compiled from a variety of surveys covering the ultraviolet, the optical, and the infrared mainly using the VizieR catalog access tool.
Proper motions were mainly taken from Gaia DR2. ... For a subsample of 15 stars, pre-Gaia proper motions measured with the HST instruments WFC3 and ACS were available.

method_chain.summary: Cross-match of MMT target list with Gaia DR2 and HST catalogs; when both Gaia and HST proper motions are available, the more precise measurement is selected (HST for HVS1, HVS10, HVS12, HVS13; Gaia for B711).

source_refs.context: Proper motions were mainly taken from Gaia DR2. ... For a subsample of 15 stars, pre-Gaia proper motions measured with the HST ... we chose the more precise measurement when Gaia as well as HST proper motions were available.

method_chain.summary: Spectroscopic analysis of flux-calibrated MMT spectra: simultaneous χ² minimization to derive effective temperature T_eff, surface gravity log(g), and projected rotational velocity v sin(i). Interstellar reddening E(B−V) included with R_V=3.1. Non-LTE model grid (Atlas12/Detail/Surface).

source_refs.context: The spectral analysis strategy basically followed Irrgang et al. (2014). ... We were thus left with four fitting parameters for the stellar spectrum: the effective temperature T_eff, the surface gravity log(g), the projected rotational velocity v sin(i), and the radial velocity v_rad.

method_chain.summary: Radial velocity v_rad measured from the same spectral fitting as step-03. The MMT spectra are fitted with the non-LTE model grid, yielding radial velocity together with atmospheric parameters.

source_refs.context: We were thus left with four fitting parameters for the stellar spectrum: the effective temperature T_eff, the surface gravity log(g), the projected rotational velocity v sin(i), and the radial velocity v_rad.

method_chain.summary: SED fitting: Observed spectral energy distributions from compiled photometry compared to synthetic SEDs (Atlas12 models) with parameters fixed to spectroscopic values. Only angular diameter Θ and color excess E(B−V) are fitted.

source_refs.context: The resulting observed SEDs were compared to synthetic ones based on Atlas12 model computations ... we were left with only two free fitting parameters, the angular diameter Θ as distance scaling factor and the color excess E(B−V) as indicator for interstellar reddening and extinction.

method_chain.summary: Spectrophotometric distances: combine angular diameters Θ = 2 R / d with stellar radii R = (G M / g)^{1/2} from spectroscopic log(g) and evolutionary masses (rotating MS models). Alternative distances for B598 (pre-ELM WD) and B576 (BHB) using typical masses.

source_refs.context: The resulting angular diameters Θ = 2 R/d were combined with the stellar radii R = (G M/g)^{1/2} which are based on the surface gravities g from spectroscopy and the stellar masses derived from evolutionary tracks ... This allowed us to calculate the spectrophotometric distances d.

method_chain.summary: Astrometric quality filter: RUWE from Gaia DR2 < 1.4 for all objects, confirming well-behaved astrometric solutions. The more precise proper motion (Gaia or HST) is selected when both are available.

source_refs.context: We used the 'renormalized unit weight error' (RUWE) ... as primary indicator for the quality of the astrometric solution. ... For all objects in the sample, RUWE is below the recommended value of 1.4, indicating that the astrometric solutions are well-behaved.

method_chain.summary: Solar position and motion parameters (R0, z0, circular velocity at the Sun, solar peculiar motion U/V/W) are not reported in this paper; they are presumably taken from Paper II (2018A&A...620A..48I).

source_refs.context: The strategy of the kinematic analysis is identical to that of Paper II.

method_chain.summary: Transformation to right-handed Cartesian Galactic coordinate system (Sun on negative x-axis, z-axis to north). Compute Galactocentric positions (x,y,z) and velocities (v_x,v_y,v_z), Galactic rest-frame velocity v_Grf, and ejection velocities (accounting for Galactic rotation).

source_refs.context: The quantities shown there are based on a right-handed Cartesian Galactic coordinate system in which the Sun is located on the negative x-axis and the z-axis points to the Galactic north pole.

method_chain.summary: Two Milky Way mass models: Model I (revision of Allen & Santillan 1991) and Model II (flat rotation curve model by Wilkinson & Evans 1999). Both calibrated with same observational constraints and consistent with Gaia DR2.

source_refs.context: Model I is a revision of the popular model by Allen & Santillan (1991) and Model II is based on the flat rotation curve model by Wilkinson & Evans (1999).

parameters.name: potential_name
parameters.raw_value: Model II (flat rotation curve model by Wilkinson & Evans 1999)

source_refs.context: Model II is based on the flat rotation curve model by Wilkinson & Evans (1999).

method_chain.summary: Backward orbit integration via Monte Carlo (1.5 million realizations) for up to 15 Gyr. Uncertainties in distance, radial velocity, and proper motions are propagated. Only orbits that cross the Galactic plane within the upper 99% confidence limit for the stellar age are retained.

source_refs.context: The strategy of the kinematic analysis is identical to that of Paper II. Complementing our revised radial velocities and spectrophotometric distances with positions and proper motions ... Propagation of uncertainties ... was achieved using a standard Monte Carlo approach with 1.5 million realizations.

method_chain.summary: Compute local escape velocity v_esc from the Galactic potential, the escape velocity excess (v_Grf − v_esc), and the bound probability P_b (as a percentage) from the Monte Carlo orbits.

Classify stars into origin categories (unconstrained, outer rim, disk origin) based on plane-crossing locations of Monte Carlo orbits; compare flight times to evolutionary ages; determine ejection velocities at plane crossing.

source_refs.context: In the following, we group the stars in three categories based on their inferred spatial origin.
Tracing back the trajectories of the targets also gave us information about their spatial origin within the Galactic plane.

inclusion_assessment.summary: HVS1 is one of the 22 HVS-numbered stars, originally unbound. Reassessment gives P_b = 0% (Model I), clearly unbound.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Bound probability is 0% in Model I; v_Grf − v_esc = 400 km/s.

source_refs.context: the boundness probability of all stars except B1080 is lower than 50%, for most of them it is even equal to zero.
extend the spectrophotometric analysis to 40 out of the 42 high-velocity outliers

inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: P_b = 0%; paper explicitly rules out GC origin.

inclusion_assessment.summary: HVS7 is an HVS-numbered star originally unbound. Possibly bound in Model I (P_b=7%), likely unbound overall. Outer-rim origin.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: P_b=7% in Model I, 0% in Model II; paper classifies as possibly bound but still unbound candidate.

source_refs.context: another four (HVS7, HVS8, HVS16, HVS17) for which this is the case at least in Model I.
For eight stars ... either stem from the outer rim of the Galactic disk or not from the disk at all

inclusion_assessment.summary: HVS8 is an HVS-numbered star originally unbound. Possibly bound in Model I (P_b=18%) but 0% in Model II. Best disk ejection candidate.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: P_b=18% in Model I, 0% in Model II; paper calls 'the best candidate for an ejection from the disk'.

source_refs.context: The best candidate for an ejection from the disk is HVS8 ... For all other stars in this group, the majority of the Monte Carlo trajectories intersects the Galactic plane outside of the 20 kpc circle.

inclusion_assessment.summary: HVS9 is an HVS-numbered star originally unbound. P_b=0%, unconstrained origin.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: P_b=0% in Model I; unconstrained origin group.

inclusion_assessment.summary: HVS10 is an HVS-numbered star originally unbound. Very likely unbound with P_b=1% in Model I, 0% in Model II. Unconstrained origin.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: P_b=1% in Model I; paper treats as still unbound candidate.

inclusion_assessment.summary: HVS11 is an original MMT HVS survey star, originally considered unbound. No proper motion measurements available, so no updated kinematic analysis. The star remains a HVS candidate.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: No kinematic reassessment due to missing proper motions.

source_refs.context: Unfortunately, proper motion measurements were not available for HVS11, HVS14, and HVS23
In this work, which we regard as Paper~III in this series, we extend the spectrophotometric analysis to 40 out of the 42 high-velocity outliers of the MMT HVS sample.

citation_context_refs.context: 40 out of the 42 high-velocity outliers of the MMT HVS sample.

inclusion_assessment.summary: HVS12 is an original HVS candidate. The kinematic analysis yields v_Grf=570 km/s, escape margin 170 km/s, bound probability 0% (Model I), placing it as unbound. Origin constrained to outer rim or beyond disk; GC excluded.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Well-constrained kinematic data with P_b=0% in both models, explicitly discussed as likely unbound.

source_refs.context: For eight stars in the sample, the precision of the kinematic analysis is sufficient to conclude that those stars either stem from the outer rim of the Galactic disk or not from the disk at all; see Fig. 8. ... The best candidate for an ejection from the disk is HVS8 ... For all other stars in this group, the majority of the Monte Carlo trajectories intersects the Galactic plane outside of the 20 kpc circle.

inclusion_assessment.summary: HVS15 is an original HVS candidate. The kinematic analysis yields v_Grf=450 km/s, escape margin 30 km/s, bound probability 41% (Model I), making it possibly bound but still a candidate for being unbound. Origin somewhat constrained, but GC excluded.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Well-constrained kinematic data, P_b=41% in Model I, paper discusses it as possibly bound but still originally unbound and candidate.

source_refs.context: It is worthwhile to also reconsider the stars originally considered unbound. We find two objects (HVS15, HVS24) that are possibly bound (P_b > 5%) in both Galactic potentials

source_refs.context: proper motion measurements were not available for HVS 11, HVS 14, and HVS 23, which is why the kinematic analysis encompasses only 37 out of the 40 stars

inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Paper states that HVS24 is possibly bound but originally considered unbound; included as candidate.

source_refs.context: We find two objects (HVS15, HVS24) that are possibly bound (P_b > 5%) in both Galactic potentials

inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Paper states B485 likely unbound in Model II (P_b=2%).

source_refs.context: Except for three stars (B485, B576, B1085), all stars originally considered bound likely remain bound ... With P_b = 2%, B485 and B1085 would likely be unbound in the lighter mass model II.
B485 is in the disk origin group with well-constrained origin

B1085 origin likely in Galactic disk
B576 row in stellar parameters table
Except for three stars (B485, B576, B1085), all stars originally considered bound likely remain bound

inclusion_assessment.summary: B576 is one of three originally bound stars that become possibly unbound in the lightest mass model. The paper finds that if it were a main-sequence star it would be clearly unbound (P_b=0%), but it is more likely a BHB star that is bound. The kinematic analysis for the MS case yields unbound status, so B576 is included as a candidate HVS under the MS assumption.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: The paper presents B576 as potentially unbound only under the MS assumption, while favoring a bound BHB nature; its inclusion as an HVS candidate is therefore ambiguous.

source_refs.context: Assuming a MS nature for B576 ... it would be the only star ... that is clearly unbound
Except for three stars (B485, B576, B1085), all stars originally considered bound likely remain bound ... B576 would be clearly unbound (P_b = 0%) in both Galactic potentials if it were a MS star
B576 would be clearly unbound ... if it were a MS star. However, it is more probable that the star is actually an evolved star of low mass that is bound to the Galaxy.

Assuming a MS nature for B576, it would be the only star in the sample for which an origin in the Galactic disk could be ruled out ... its current Galactic rest-frame velocity ... would then render the object clearly unbound

distance.description: Spectrophotometric distance under the MS assumption from Table 3 (stellar_params)

radial_velocity.description: Radial velocity from spectroscopic analysis (Table 1, statistical uncertainties +1.9/-1.7 km/s, systematic +0.4/-0.3 km/s). Only the central value is stored.

escape_margin.description: Galactic rest-frame velocity minus local escape velocity, v_Grf - v_esc

bound_probability.description: Bound probability P_b (Model I) from kinematic table; 0%.

spectroscopy.description: Projected rotational velocity v sin(i) from spectroscopic analysis (statistical errors +12/-13 km/s, systematic +8/-8 km/s).

teff.description: Effective temperature from spectroscopic analysis (statistical errors +30/-30 K, systematic +120/-120 K)

log_g.description: Surface gravity log(g) from spectroscopic analysis (statistical errors +0.02/-0.02, systematic +0.04/-0.04)

luminosity.description: Logarithm of stellar luminosity relative to solar

quality_flags.description: Gaia DR2 astrometric quality: all objects have RUWE below the recommended value of 1.4, indicating well-behaved astrometric solutions.

source_refs.context: For all objects in the sample, RUWE is below the recommended value of 1.4, indicating that the astrometric solutions are well-behaved.

quality_flags.name: RUWE

pericenter.description: Galactocentric distance at the point where the orbit crossed the Galactic plane (r_p from kinematic table).

flight_time.description: Flight time from the Galactic plane to current position (τ_flight,p)

origin_site.description: Under the MS assumption, the star is unbound and likely just passing through the Galaxy, implying an extragalactic origin.

source_refs.context: its current Galactic rest-frame velocity ... would then render the object clearly unbound, which would imply that it were just passing through our Milky Way.

ejection_velocity.description: Ejection velocity at plane crossing, corrected for Galactic rotation (v_ej,p)

extra.description: Spectrophotometric distance under the BHB assumption (BHB mass 0.5 M_sun).

source_refs.context: We furthermore examined the BHB case for candidate B576 and determined d=18.7^{+1.6}_{-1.5} kpc based on the assumption of a typical BHB mass of M=0.5 M_sun.

extra.name: BHB_distance

candidate_groups_considered.description: 22 stars with HVS designations (HVS1, HVS4–HVS24) from the MMT HVS survey, all with re-analyzed spectra and spectrophotometric distances; 19 of them have full kinematic analysis.
candidate_groups_considered.reason: The paper treats all HVS-numbered stars as originally unbound candidates. None are conclusively shown to be bound; even HVS15 and HVS24 are only 'possibly bound', and others are clearly unbound. They are included as possibly unbound.

source_refs.context: The original MMT HVS sample identified 42 B-type stars whose Galactic rest-frame radial velocity exceeds +275 km s⁻¹, sixteen of which were considered bound to the Galaxy as indicated by their name starting with the letter 'B'.

It is worthwhile to also reconsider the stars originally considered unbound. We find two objects (HVS15, HVS24) that are possibly bound (P_b > 5%) in both Galactic potentials and another four (HVS7, HVS8, HVS16, HVS17) for which this is the case at least in Model I.

candidate_groups_considered.description: 17 B-numbered stars originally classified as bound runaway stars (B095, B129, B143, B167, B329, B434, B458, B481, B537, B572, B598, B711, B733, B1080, B1139, B485, B1085, B576). Three (B485, B1085, B576) are found possibly unbound; the other 15 remain bound.
candidate_groups_considered.reason: The paper concludes that all B-numbered stars except B485, B1085, and B576 remain likely bound (bound probability >5% in both Galactic mass models) and are therefore not treated as possibly unbound. Those 15 stars are excluded; the three exceptions are included individually.

source_refs.context: Except for three stars (B485, B576, B1085), all stars originally considered bound likely remain bound (probability P_b > 5%) irrespective of the choice of the Galactic mass model.
The original MMT HVS sample identified 42 B-type stars ... sixteen of which were considered bound to the Galaxy as indicated by their name starting with the letter 'B'.
